questions.question_text: When is a Nevada licensee required to obtain a signed Consent to Act form?
questions.correct_answer: Before acting for more than one party to the transaction
distractors: Before performing any acts for any new client
At the first meeting with a prospective buyer
Whenever a brokerage agreement is signed
questions.explanation: The Consent to Act (NRED Form 524) is required only when a licensee acts for more than one party in the same transaction. NRS 645.252(1)(d) requires the licensee to disclose the multiple representation and obtain each party's written consent before continuing to act as an agent. Routine single-client relationships require the Duties Owed form, not a Consent to Act.

questions.question_text: A Nevada licensee proposes to represent both the seller and the buyer in the same transaction. What does the signed Consent to Act form establish?
questions.correct_answer: Each party's written consent to the dual representation
distractors: The commission rate to be paid
An employment agreement with both parties
The geographic area of representation
questions.explanation: Nevada permits a licensee to act for more than one party only after obtaining each party's written consent, and NRED Form 524 is that consent. The form states it 'does not constitute a contract for services nor an agreement to pay compensation,' so it sets no employment or commission terms — those belong in separate brokerage agreements.

questions.question_text: After disclosing that she will act for both buyer and seller, a licensee continues the transaction without obtaining each party's written consent. What is the licensee's status?
questions.correct_answer: In violation of Nevada licensing law
distractors: Compliant, because oral disclosure is sufficient
Automatically acting as an assigned agent
Representing the seller by default
questions.explanation: Under NRS 645.252(1)(d), a licensee who acts for more than one party must obtain the written consent of each party before continuing to act as an agent — disclosure alone is not enough. The written Consent to Act (Form 524) must be signed by the parties. No Consent to Act is needed when a licensee represents only one party.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Nevada's Consent to Act form is TRUE?
questions.correct_answer: It is not an employment agreement
distractors: It must be notarized to be valid
It guarantees the licensee a commission
It can only be used for sellers
questions.explanation: The Consent to Act form itself states that it 'does not constitute a contract for services nor an agreement to pay compensation.' It does not need to be notarized and does not guarantee compensation. It is used whenever a licensee acts for more than one party — both the seller/landlord and the buyer/tenant sign it. Employment and compensation terms are set by separate brokerage agreements.

questions.question_text: A licensee plans to represent both buyer and seller with their consent. What must the Consent to Act form disclose?
questions.correct_answer: That the licensee has a conflict of interest
distractors: That the seller will pay both commissions
That neither party will have representation
That the transaction requires an attorney
questions.explanation: Under NRS 645.252(1)(d), the written consent must describe the transaction and state that the licensee is acting for two or more parties with adverse interests and, in doing so, has a conflict of interest. It must also state that no party is required to consent and that consent is given without coercion. Nevada law does not use the term 'limited agent' — the required disclosure is the conflict of interest.

questions.question_text: When must a Nevada licensee provide the Duties Owed by a Nevada Real Estate Licensee form?
questions.correct_answer: As soon as practicable, before any transaction document is signed
distractors: Only after an offer is accepted
Within 48 hours of the first showing
At the close of escrow
questions.explanation: NAC 645.637 requires the disclosure to be made 'as soon as practicable, but not later than the date and time on which any written document is signed' by the client or an unrepresented party. NRED confirms this covers listing agreements, purchase agreements, leases, letters of intent, and buyer brokerage agreements. Nevada has no separate agency form issued at the time an offer is made.

questions.question_text: To whom must a Nevada licensee provide the Duties Owed form?
questions.correct_answer: Each client and each unrepresented party
distractors: Only the licensee's own client
Every party, including those represented by other licensees
Only the seller in the transaction
questions.explanation: NRS 645.252(3) requires a licensee to provide the Division's Duties Owed form to each party for whom the licensee is acting as an agent and to each unrepresented party, if any. A party represented by another licensee receives the form from that party's own agent, not from every licensee in the transaction.

questions.question_text: A broker assigns one affiliated licensee to the seller and a different affiliated licensee to the buyer in the same transaction. What written consent does Nevada require?
questions.correct_answer: None — assigned licensees need no Consent to Act
distractors: A Consent to Act signed by both parties
A Confirmation of Agency Relationship at the offer
Notarized consent from both brokers
questions.explanation: Under NRS 645.253, when a broker assigns different licensees affiliated with the brokerage to separate parties, the licensees are not required to obtain the written consent that NRS 645.252(1)(d) requires when a single licensee acts for both parties. Each assigned licensee still must not disclose a client's confidential information except to the broker.

questions.question_text: What does a party's signature on Nevada's Duties Owed form acknowledge?
questions.correct_answer: Receipt and understanding of the disclosure
distractors: Creation of an agency relationship with the licensee
Agreement to pay the licensee's compensation
Waiver of the right to independent representation
questions.explanation: Form 525's signature block reads: 'I/We acknowledge receipt of a copy of this list of licensee duties, and have read and understand this disclosure.' NRED Bulletin 34 stresses that the form is a disclosure and does not create an agency relationship, and the form itself states it is not a contract for services or an agreement to pay compensation.

questions.question_text: How does the Duties Owed form differ from the Consent to Act form in Nevada?
questions.correct_answer: Duties Owed goes to clients and unrepresented parties; Consent to Act covers dual representation
distractors: Consent to Act replaces the Duties Owed form
Duties Owed is required only in dual agency
Both forms create binding employment agreements
questions.explanation: The Duties Owed form (Form 525) must be provided by each licensee to the party the licensee represents and to any unrepresented party, no later than the signing of any transaction document (NAC 645.637). The Consent to Act (Form 524) is required only when one licensee acts for more than one party (NRS 645.252(1)(d)). Neither form is a contract for services or an agreement to pay compensation.
